chest.name: Barbell Flat Bench Press
chest.muscle_group: Primary: Mid and Lower Pecs, Secondary: Triceps and Front Delts.
chest.description: Science treats this as the "100% baseline" for chest activation. It allows for the highest mechanical tension (lifting the most weight)

chest.name: Incline Dumbbell Press
chest.muscle_group: Primary: Upper Pecs (Clavicular Head) ,Secondary: Front Delts and Triceps.
chest.description: While the flat bench is King, the incline press is what builds the "shelf" look. Using dumbbells provides a greater range of motion and prevents your stronger side from doing all the work.

chest.name: Pec Deck Fly
chest.muscle_group: Primary: Full Pectoralis Major , Secondary: Minimal Front Deltoids.
chest.description: According to ACE research, this exercise is 98% as effective as the barbell bench press but with much lower risk and no need for a spotter.

chest.name: Weighted Dips
chest.muscle_group: Primary: Lower Pecs , Secondary: Triceps and Front Delts.
chest.description: Often called the "bodyweight bench press", dips are elite for targeting the lower portion of the pectoralis major.

chest.name: Cable Crossover
chest.muscle_group: Primary: Lower and Inner Pecs , Secondary: Front Deltoids.
chest.description: Cables offer something gravity-based weights don't: Constant Tension. In a dumbbell fly, tension disappears at the top; in cables, it stays 100% throughout the movement.

back.name: Barbell Rows
back.muscle_group: Primary: Latissimus Dorsi and Rhomboids ,Secondary: Biceps and Rear Delts.
back.description: A staple for adding back thickness. It requires a strong static hold in the hamstrings and lower back while pulling heavy weight toward the hip.

back.name: Pull-Ups
back.muscle_group: Primary: Latissimus Dorsi ,Secondary: Biceps, Forearms, and Traps.
back.description: The ultimate bodyweight test for back width. Research suggests wide-grip variations maximize lat activation.

back.name: Lat Pulldown
back.muscle_group: Primary: Latissimus Dorsi ,Secondary: Teres Major and Biceps.
back.description: Excellent for isolating the lats without the grip fatigue of a pull-up. Allows for controlled eccentric movement which is great for hypertrophy.

back.name: Seated Cable Row
back.muscle_group: Primary: Rhomboids and Lower Traps ,Secondary: Lats and Biceps.
back.description: Provides constant tension through the entire horizontal pull. Great for improving posture by strengthening the mid-back.

back.name: Single Arm Dumbbell Row
back.muscle_group: Primary: Latissimus Dorsi ,Secondary: Rhomboids and Brachialis.
back.description: Allows for a deeper stretch at the bottom and a higher pull at the top compared to barbell versions. Helps fix muscle imbalances.

legs.name: Romanian Deadlift
legs.muscle_group: Primary: Hamstrings and Glutes ,Secondary: Lower Back and Forearms.
legs.description: Focuses on the hinge movement. It is the best exercise for hamstring hypertrophy due to the extreme weighted stretch.

legs.name: Leg Press
legs.description: Allows you to move massive weight without worrying about balance or lower back stability. Great for pure quad exhaustion.

legs.name: Bulgarian Split Squat
legs.muscle_group: Primary: Quads and Glutes ,Secondary: Core and Stabilizers.
legs.description: Famous for being incredibly painful but effective. It isolates each leg to ensure symmetrical development.

legs.name: Leg Extensions
legs.description: The only movement that fully isolates the quads. Perfect for finishing a workout and achieving a massive pump.

legs.name: Lying Leg Curl
legs.description: Isolates the hamstrings through knee flexion. Keeping the hips pressed down is key for maximum activation.

shoulders.name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
shoulders.description: The #1 exercise for shoulder width. Small weights are usually better here to maintain strict form and isolate the side delt.

shoulders.name: Face Pulls
shoulders.muscle_group: Primary: Rear Deltoids and Traps ,Secondary: Rotator Cuff.
shoulders.description: Essential for shoulder health and posture. It targets the often-neglected rear deltoids.

shoulders.name: Arnold Press
shoulders.muscle_group: Primary: Front and Side Deltoids ,Secondary: Triceps.
shoulders.description: Created by Arnold Schwarzenegger. The rotation during the lift increases the time under tension for the front and side delts.

shoulders.name: Bent Over Rear Delt Fly
shoulders.description: Isolates the posterior deltoid. Important for creating a 3D look to the shoulder from the side and back.

shoulders.name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
shoulders.muscle_group: Primary: Front Deltoids ,Secondary: Triceps and Side Delts.
shoulders.description: A seated version allows for more stability and heavier focus on the shoulders without involving the legs or lower back.

arms.name: Tricep Pushdown
arms.description: High-repetition staple for tricep growth. Using a rope attachment allows for a greater range of motion at the bottom.

arms.name: Skull Crushers
arms.description: Targets the long head of the tricep which is the largest part of the arm. Stretching the muscle under load is the key here.

arms.name: Hammer Curls
arms.description: A neutral grip targets the 'width' of the arm. It builds the muscle that sits under the bicep, pushing it up higher.

arms.name: EZ Bar Preacher Curl
arms.description: Eliminates momentum by locking your arms against a bench. It forces the biceps to do 100% of the work.

arms.name: Close Grip Bench Press
arms.muscle_group: Primary: Triceps ,Secondary: Chest and Front Delts.
arms.description: The best heavy compound lift for triceps. Moving the hands closer together shifts the mechanical load from the chest to the arms.

core.name: Plank
core.muscle_group: Primary: Transverse Abdominis and Rectus Abdominis ,Secondary: Lower Back and Shoulders.
core.description: An isometric hold that builds deep core stability. It is the foundation for protecting the spine during heavy lifts.

core.name: Ab Wheel Rollout
core.muscle_group: Primary: Rectus Abdominis ,Secondary: Lats and Shoulders.
core.description: Extremely high eccentric load on the abs. One of the most effective movements for core strength according to EMG studies.

core.name: Russian Twists
core.description: Targets the rotational strength of the core. Essential for athletes who need power in twisting movements.

core.name: Cable Woodchoppers
core.description: Provides diagonal resistance. It mimics real-world movements and is elite for developing a strong, tapered waist.

core.name: Cable Crunches
core.description: One of the few ab exercises where you can easily add weight. To grow abs like any other muscle, you need progressive overload.
